critic_raw: The design uses 'timestamped article access logs' to infer which articles agents consulted. But agents may access articles to read and dismiss as irrelevant — an access log doesn't distinguish between 'consulted and used' vs. 'consulted and rejected.' For Recall@10 evaluation, articles that were accessed but rejected should not be positive labels. The manual review of 200-300 tickets to 'verify label quality' may not scale to identify this pattern systematically across all tickets.
defender_raw: The design uses 'explicit selection in the ticketing system or timestamped article access logs' — with preference for explicit selection (tickets where agents actively linked an article to the resolution). The timestamped access logs are a secondary signal for tickets without explicit selection. The expert review of 200-300 tickets directly tests for 'cases where multiple articles are legitimately correct' and label noise. If the expert review identifies a significant pattern of accessed-but-rejected articles, the design can filter to explicit selections only for the primary evaluation. Both models are evaluated on the same label set; any noise applies symmetrically.
adjudication_raw: One concern: article access logs don't distinguish consulted-and-used vs. consulted-and-rejected. Addressed: explicit selection is preferred over access logs; expert review diagnoses label noise; both models evaluated on same labels. Verdict: defense_wins.
